beats.text: Sound is a one-dimensional pressure signal that a microphone samples at a fixed rate into a stream of amplitudes.
One second of 16 kHz mono audio is 16,000 numbers, so raw waveforms are faithful but punishingly long.
The Short-Time Fourier Transform slides a window across the signal and takes the Fourier transform of each frame.
A short window localizes when a sound happened, a long window resolves which frequency, but their product stays fixed.
Human hearing is roughly logarithmic, so spectrograms are warped onto the mel scale into about 80 perceptual bands.
Neural codecs like EnCodec use residual vector quantization to turn the waveform into a grid of discrete tokens.

visual.note: Stacked codebooks give K^Q combinations at Q log2 K bits

beats.text: Automatic speech recognition turns audio into text, once a hand-glued chain of acoustic, pronunciation, and language models.
Whisper is a plain encoder-decoder transformer trained on 680,000 hours of weakly labelled, multilingual web audio.
Audio is resampled to 16 kHz, converted to an 80-bin log-mel spectrogram over 30-second chunks, and fed to the encoder.
The decoder autoregressively predicts text tokens, conditioning each one on the audio encoding and the text emitted so far.

visual.note: A translator whose source language is sound

beats.text: Robustness came from scale and weak supervision, teaching it to handle accents, noise, and code-switching.
The cost is hallucination, since on silent or unintelligible audio the decoder confidently invents fluent text.
Text-to-speech runs recognition in reverse: text in, waveform out, rebuilt as two learned stages joined by the spectrogram.
The acoustic model maps text to a log-mel spectrogram, then a vocoder maps that spectrogram to samples.

visual.note: The spectrogram is a deliberate bottleneck

beats.text: Tacotron 2 reads characters and emits mel frames with attention, but could skip or repeat words when alignment slipped.
FastSpeech 2 predicts an explicit duration per token and expands in one shot, trading naturalness for solid alignment.
WaveNet models raw audio one sample at a time with dilated causal convolutions whose receptive field grows exponentially.

visual.note: Dilations 1, 2, 4, ... cover thousands of samples cheaply

beats.text: WaveNet's quality was a leap but its serial speed was a disaster, so GAN vocoders now generate a waveform in one pass.

visual.label: Samples generated per second, one at a time

beats.text: Once a codec turns audio into discrete tokens, the boundary between speech and language dissolves.
An audio language model predicts the next audio token exactly as a text model predicts the next word.

visual.note: Only the vocabulary changed, from words to RVQ codes

beats.text: AudioLM continues a few seconds of speech or piano, preserving speaker identity, prosody, and even room acoustics.
It splits tokens into coarse semantic content and fine acoustic detail, modelling meaning before texture.
VALL-E clones a voice from a three-second sample, and omni models emit audio with no separate ASR or TTS stage.

visual.label: Seconds of reference for zero-shot cloning

beats.text: Audio tokens are dense, so context windows fill fast and long-form coherence remains hard.
A spoken-conversation assistant lives or dies on latency, since the cascade of stages easily blows past a full second.
Humans tolerate roughly 200 to 300 milliseconds of silence before a reply starts to feel unnatural.
Streaming ASR processes small overlapping chunks and emits partial transcripts as words arrive, never waiting for the end.
Blocking cascades add the latency of every stage, while streaming overlaps them so only the first tokens matter.

visual.tex: L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{ASR}} + L_{\text{LLM}} + L_{\text{TTS}} \xrightarrow{\text{streaming}} \max\big(L_{\text{ASR}}^{\text{first}}, L_{\text{TTS}}^{\text{first}}\big) + L_{\text{LLM}}^{\text{first}}
visual.note: Native audio models make the ASR and TTS hops vanish

beats.text: The hardest problems are conversational: end-of-turn detection, barge-in, and modelling backchannels like mm-hmm.
Moshi listens and speaks at once over parallel token streams, dropping perceived latency toward 200 milliseconds.
